prompt: Operating-system services split into two groups. Which pair names them correctly?
solution: The slides divide OS services into those (a) helpful to the user — user interface, program execution, I/O, file-system manipulation, communications, error detection — and (b) helpful to the system itself — resource allocation, accounting, and protection & security.

prompt: Error detection is classified as a service that is helpful to the user.
solution: True. Error detection sits in the user-helpful group: the OS stays aware of errors in the CPU, memory, I/O devices, and user programs and takes appropriate action, with debugging facilities to help. Resource allocation, accounting, and protection & security are the system-helpful services.

prompt: Which of the following is a service that is helpful to the system, not directly to the user?
solution: Resource allocation — dividing CPU cycles, memory, storage, and I/O among concurrent users and jobs — is a system-helpful service. Program execution, file-system manipulation, and I/O operations are all user-helpful.

prompt: The desktop metaphor used by modern graphical user interfaces was invented at:
solution: The GUI desktop metaphor — icons representing files, programs, and actions, driven by a pointing device — was invented at Xerox PARC.

prompt: When a shell's commands are just the names of programs (rather than built in), adding a new command requires modifying the shell.
solution: False. When commands are the names of programs, adding a new feature does NOT require changing the shell — you just add a program the shell can find and run. Only built-in commands are part of the shell itself.

prompt: What happens to the CPU when a running program makes a system call?
choices: It stays in user mode the whole time
It switches from user mode to kernel mode, then back
It shuts down until the call completes
It switches permanently to kernel mode
solution: A system call switches the CPU from user mode to kernel mode (a context switch) so the kernel can perform the privileged operation, then switches back to user mode when the call returns.

prompt: Which API is used by UNIX, Linux, and macOS?
solution: The POSIX API covers virtually all versions of UNIX, Linux, and macOS. Win32 is the Windows API, and the Java API targets the Java Virtual Machine.

prompt: Programs usually call a high-level API (like the C library's read()) instead of invoking the system call directly. The main benefit is:
choices: Faster raw execution than the system call
Portability and decoupling the program from kernel specifics
It gives the program access to more CPU registers
solution: The API is the function you call in your code; it in turn invokes the real system call. Programming to the API keeps your code decoupled from kernel details and makes it portable across systems that offer the same API.

prompt: Which parameter-passing method stores the parameters in a memory block and passes the block's address in a register (as Linux and Solaris do)?
solution: In the block (table) method, parameters are stored in a block in memory and the address of that block is passed in a register. Linux and Solaris use this approach; unlike plain registers, it doesn't limit the number or length of parameters.

prompt: Which parameter-passing method does Windows use, with the program pushing parameters that the OS then pops?
solution: The stack method has the program push parameters onto the stack; the operating system pops them off. Windows uses this. Like the block method, it isn't limited by the number of available registers.

prompt: The calls fork(), exit(), and wait() belong to which system-call category?
solution: Creating and terminating processes, loading and executing programs, waiting for events, and allocating memory are all process control. File management covers open/read/write; device management covers request/release/read/write of devices.

prompt: Getting the time or date, or reading and setting system data, falls into which category of system call?
solution: Information maintenance covers getting/setting the time or date, getting/setting system data, and querying or setting process, file, and device attributes.

prompt: After a successful fork(), what value does the call return in the child process?
solution: fork() returns twice: 0 in the child, and the child's PID in the parent. That difference in return value is exactly how each process knows which one it is (the basis for the if (pid == 0) split).

prompt: By convention, what exit code does a UNIX process return to indicate that it finished with no error?
solution: An exit code of 0 means no error. Any code greater than 0 is an error code that the parent can read from wait().

prompt: In one sentence, distinguish what fork() does from what exec() does.
answer: fork() creates a new process as a copy of the caller, while exec() replaces a process's memory image with a new program.
solution: fork() clones the calling process, producing a parent and a near-identical child. exec() then loads a different program into the child, replacing its memory image — which is why exec() never returns on success.

prompt: Which OS structure moves as much as possible out of the kernel into user space, with modules communicating by message passing?
solution: A microkernel keeps the kernel minimal (mainly inter-process communication) and pushes services like the file server and device drivers into user space, where they communicate by message passing through the microkernel. Mach is the classic example.

prompt: What is the main drawback of a microkernel design?
choices: It is impossible to port to new architectures
Performance overhead from user-space to kernel-space message passing
It cannot be extended with new services
It runs every service in kernel mode
solution: Microkernels are easier to extend, port, and secure (less code runs in kernel mode), but the cost is performance overhead: services now communicate across the user/kernel boundary by message passing instead of direct calls.

prompt: The Linux kernel is a pure monolithic design with no support for loadable modules.
solution: False. Linux is monolithic PLUS modular — it runs in a single kernel address space but supports loadable kernel modules for dynamic functionality. Most modern kernels are hybrids of several structures.

prompt: Background services (daemons) are best described as:
choices: Programs the user runs interactively in the foreground
Programs launched at boot — some run from boot to shutdown, providing facilities like disk checking, logging, and printing
Entries in the kernel's interrupt vector
System calls in the communications category
solution: Background services launch at boot time. Some run only for system startup and then terminate; others run from boot to shutdown, providing facilities such as disk checking, process scheduling, error logging, and printing.

prompt: What firmware interface has replaced the traditional BIOS in modern systems?
solution: Modern systems replace the BIOS with UEFI (Unified Extensible Firmware Interface). GRUB is a common bootstrap loader (which selects and loads the kernel); EEPROM is a type of firmware storage, not the interface.

prompt: A failure of the operating system itself can generate which kind of dump, and what does it contain?
choices: A core dump containing the kernel's memory
A crash dump containing kernel memory
A core dump containing a single process's memory
A crash dump containing one process's memory
solution: An OS failure generates a crash dump containing kernel memory. A core dump is the counterpart for an application failure — it captures that single process's memory.

prompt: What is profiling, as used in performance tuning?
answer: Profiling periodically samples the instruction pointer to look for statistical trends in where a program spends its time.
solution: Profiling is periodic sampling of the instruction pointer to reveal statistical trends — for example, which functions consume the most time — so bottlenecks can be found and tuned. Trace listings, which record activity for later analysis, are a related technique.
